steps.voice: First boot — the dash auto-scans CAN at four common bitrates: 125, 250, 500 and 1000 kilobits per second. With the ECU powered and broadcasting, this usually locks on in two to four seconds.

Lock-on confirmed — tap Apply to commit the matched bitrate. If the scan times out, swap CAN-H and CAN-L at the connector (the most common mistake), or jump down to the diagnostics section at the bottom of this page.

Pick your ECU. Built-in presets cover MaxxECU, Haltech Nexus and Elite, Link G4 plus, Ford BA / BF / FG, ECUMaster, MegaSquirt, and a GM GEN IV LS profile. Don't see yours? Pick Custom — you can also DBC-import later.
Haltech Nexus / Elite is pre-selected. Apply.

Three ways to reach the web editor — Home Wi-Fi if you've got signal in the garage, USB cable for the desktop Studio app, or the dash's own hotspot when you're off-grid. Pick whichever matches your setup.
Mode on the left, networks on the right. Pick Home 2.4.
Connected. The dash's IP is now in Device Settings — there's a Show QR button there too, point your phone camera at it and the editor opens automatically.
And this is what you've set up — live CAN data rendered through the default layout.
You're ready to drive. Long-press the RDM logo any time to reach Device Settings, or two-finger tap to flip night mode.
